title: The AI Race Just Got a SpaceX-Sized Twist

XAI is being acquired by SpaceX
OpenAI's new Codex app and the shifting paradigm of how we work with agents.

mentions.context_snippet: Instead, argues the Information's Martin Peers, there's no question the move is financially motivated.
Bloomberg reported this month that XAI recorded a net loss of $1.46 billion for the September quarter on $107 million in revenue.

mentions.quoted_text: X was out of money merged with XAI, XAI out of money merged with SpaceX, SpaceX out of money merged with Tesla, when they are all out of money, dot, dot, dot.
mentions.context_snippet: Investor Ross Gerber summed up one frequently shared skeptical take. He tweeted, X was out of money merged with XAI...

mentions.quoted_text: An 80-20 split for SpaceX and XAI seems like a horrible deal for SpaceX. Giving the number 5, if that, AI company with limited real revenue, and Twitter, ownership of 20% of a truly world-changing rocket company is ridiculously bad for SpaceX shareholders. SpaceX doesn't need XAI, XAI does need SpaceX.
mentions.context_snippet: Compound248 asked how SpaceX investors are going to feel about this. They wrote, An 80-20 split for SpaceX and XAI seems like a horrible deal for SpaceX.

mentions.quoted_text: mom-how-did-we-get-so-rich meme with the answer, Your dad worked at Twitter which got acquired by X which got acquired by XAI which got acquired by SpaceX.
mentions.context_snippet: Nate McGrady posted the mom-how-did-we-get-so-rich meme with the answer, Your dad worked at Twitter...

mentions.quoted_text: Elon says that within 2-3 years the lowest cost way to generate AI compute will be in space. What's the Elon to reality multiple on that estimate?
mentions.context_snippet: Steve Howell of Bloomberg tweets, Elon says that within 2-3 years the lowest cost way to generate AI compute will be in space.

mentions.quoted_text: Does anyone believe this nonsense?
mentions.context_snippet: Nick Carter shared the portion of the post where Elon wrote, In the long term, space-based AI is obviously the only way to scale.

mentions.quoted_text: SpaceX can turn fuel into solar energy. Intelligence turns energy into economic value. SpaceX and XAI is the ultimate way to turn rocket fuel into unlimited revenue.
mentions.context_snippet: Beth Jezos summed up that optimism in his post, SpaceX can turn fuel into solar energy...

mentions.quoted_text: Did Sam Altman commit the biggest theft in world history? Elon Musk finally got revenge today by bringing SpaceX into XAI. All investment liquidity will be redirected from OpenAI into XAI leaving Sam with nothing. A 5D chess move to destroy Sam and Elon made the final blow.
mentions.context_snippet: The Financealot account, meanwhile, explored what it might mean for OpenAI. They write, Did Sam Altman commit the biggest theft in world history?

Just a couple of days ago, they officially launched Grok Imagine 1.0. The update brings 10 second video generation, 720p resolution, and as they put it, dramatically better audio, and a lot of people are impressed.

Blitzy solves this with infinite code context, understanding your codebase down to the line level dependency across millions of lines of code.
Anthropic and Claude have seemed like an unassailable juggernaut when it comes to coding-related use cases.
all the way going back to Sonnet 3.5.
it's been very clear ever since the launch of GPT-5, frankly, that OpenAI was not going to give up without a fight.

mentions.quoted_text: Codex app is out for Mac. I'm surprised by how much I love it. It's a bigger step forward than I imagined.
mentions.context_snippet: Sam Altman even recently recognized that part of the reason that 5.2 wasn't very good at writing is that they just deprioritized that use case versus everything relating to code.
One of the things that happened with the launch of Claude Code about a year ago now is that developer behavior shifted away from graphical user interfaces and towards the terminal.
which is why we are introducing the Codex Desktop app, a command center for agents.

mentions.quoted_text: Today, we're introducing the Codex app, our flagship Codex experience. Work on multiple things in parallel, extend Codex with skills, and automate repetitive tasks. The most exciting part for us has been using the app to build itself.
mentions.context_snippet: Andrew Ambrosino writes, Today, we're introducing the Codex app, our flagship Codex experience.

mentions.quoted_text: Codex now pretty much builds itself with the help and supervision of a great team. The bottleneck has shifted to being how fast we can help and supervise the outcome.
mentions.context_snippet: Tebow on the Codex team writes, Codex now pretty much builds itself with the help and supervision of a great team.

mentions.quoted_text: I've been a diehard Terminal and Emacs user for many years, but since using the Codex app, going back to the Terminal has felt like going back in time. Feels like an agent-native interface for building.
mentions.context_snippet: President Greg Brockman wrote, I've been a diehard Terminal and Emacs user for many years, but since using the Codex app...
Prominent AI encoding YouTuber and content creator Theo dropped a 22-minute video about how much he loved the product
For him, the comparison is clearly not just to Claude Code, but also to Cursor, with him even calling it a Cursor killer.

mentions.mention_text: Swix and the team at Layton Space
mentions.context_snippet: Swix and the team at Layton Space said, We almost did not give OpenAI the title story today.
The team at Every did one of their classic vibe checks and basically found Codex was good.

mentions.quoted_text: previously I was using Claude Code 80% of the time and Codex 20% of the time. Over the last few weeks in the app, that percentage has become 50-50.
mentions.context_snippet: Every founder Dan Shipper writes, previously I was using Claude Code 80% of the time and Codex 20% of the time.
Opus 4.5 is still my daily driver for the rest of my work, and for programming tasks that require taste, empathy, and speed, but the reversal is significant.
On Sunday, Yuchen Jin wrote, the creator of Claudebot slash Moltbot slash OpenClaw, Peter Steinberger, pushes 144 commits per day on average.

mentions.quoted_text: I don't let Claude code on my codebase. It's all Codex. Would be too buggy with Opus.
mentions.context_snippet: Peter bit back, kinda harshly, I don't let Claude code on my codebase. It's all Codex.
in part because of the success of OpenClaw.
mentions.review_reason: Unclear if OpenClaw is a product, model, or other.

mentions.quoted_text: It's interesting, he writes, how the Codex app has increased people's usage of Codex.
mentions.context_snippet: Daniel Hassan points out that even with just the early reactions, it does seem like interface makes a difference.

mentions.quoted_text: The pattern keeps repeating. Cloud Code dropped and everyone filed it under DevTools for months. Then people realized it was a general-purpose computer agent wearing a coding hat. Codex just launched a proper desktop app. Same energy. It's not a coding tool, it's an agent command center. Parallel threads across projects. Browser automation. Skills library. 30-minute autonomous runs. The coding angle is just the entry point. If you're waiting for someone to explicitly announce, this is the everything agent, you're going to keep being late.
mentions.context_snippet: The anonymous I Rule the World account on Twitter writes, The pattern keeps repeating. Cloud Code dropped and everyone filed it under DevTools for months.

mentions.quoted_text: the features will easily be copied to all other agent managers in weeks.
mentions.context_snippet: Chris Cinco writes, the features will easily be copied to all other agent managers in weeks.

mentions.quoted_text: curious how all the Vibecode startups are doing with the Claude Code Explosion and now a very user-friendly Codex app.
mentions.context_snippet: Gavin Purcell writes, curious how all the Vibecode startups are doing with the Claude Code Explosion and now a very user-friendly Codex app.

mentions.quoted_text: Big identity crisis in many engineering circles right now, he writes. People who've historically considered themselves builders now realizing they aren't the ones building anything anymore. AI is.
mentions.context_snippet: Nick St. Pierre echoed something similar. Big identity crisis in many engineering circles right now, he writes.

mentions.quoted_text: AI is going to sever the deepest identity loop in the West, i.e. who you are is roughly equal to what you do for money. For centuries, trade was dignity, meaning social rank and even morality. Capitalism wielded cognition to occupation. What do you do became shorthand for what are you worth. Now, machines are almost done encroaching this almost sacred layer for almost all white collar work. The next few years will be like watching God being forced to retire in real time.
mentions.context_snippet: Signal reposted that and said, Nick St. Pierre echoed something similar. Big identity crisis in many engineering circles right now, he writes.
All signs continue to point to Claude Sonnet 5 coming very soon.

new_type_candidates.reason: Colossus clusters are described as major infrastructure for AI compute, not a software product or model.

notes: Some entities like Multbook, OpenClaw, Granola, Dovetail, Ashby, and Clulee need further review to confirm if they are software products, models, or organizations.
Colossus clusters and Kardashev level 2 civilization are included as 'other' due to lack of a more precise locked type.
All URLs are omitted unless explicitly stated or sponsor/official site.
Sentiment is based on context and quoted text where available.
Sponsor reads are marked as is_editorial=false.
No duplicate mention/context pairs are included.
